title: Quorum
shortDescription: A sector-aware investment advising platform that uses a council of LLM advisors and real-time public sentiment to turn noisy financial signals into clear Buy, Hold, or Avoid guidance.
problemStatement: Traditional investment advising often lacks sector-aware, diverse perspectives and fails to dynamically integrate real-time public sentiment. Investors are overwhelmed by mixed signals and struggle to make informed decisions. The challenge was to create a solution that provides clear, actionable guidance while reducing cognitive overload for retail investors and advisors.

steps.title: Conceptualization & Design
steps.description: Conceptualized the platform in collaboration with Persistent Innovation Labs and MSOE AI Club. The primary goal was to build a tool that turns noisy financial signals into decision-ready guidance, reducing cognitive overload for retail investors and advisors.

steps.title: Investment Council Dashboard
steps.description: Developed the Investment Council dashboard, allowing users to compare multiple stock tickers side-by-side (such as MSFT and NVDA). The dashboard generates a council confidence score and clear Buy, Hold, or Avoid ratings powered by a specialized team of AI advisors (Risk, Growth, Fundamentals, Sector Strategist, and Sentiment).

steps.title: Auditable AI Reasoning
steps.description: Ensured the system was 'Auditable by Design' to avoid black-box AI outputs. Each advisor provides transparent reasoning modals displaying underlying ML model signals, conviction percentages, and key financial metrics like log-returns, volatility, and moving average outperformance.

steps.title: Industry Ready Architecture
steps.description: Architected the software to be 'Fit for Industry', structuring data ingestion pipelines to easily support plugins for industry-standard platforms like Bloomberg, PitchBook, and S&P Global. The solution uses a council of LLM advisors that deliberate on a stock ticker with data from YFinance, plus a customer sentiment advisor powered by the Alpha Vantage API.

title: Brain Aneurysm Detection & Segmentation
shortDescription: Developed a two-stage 3D deep learning pipeline for automated aneurysm detection in CTA scans, achieving 89.6% detection sensitivity. Selected to present at MICS 2026 and the MSOE Rosie Competition poster session.
problemStatement: As part of an MSOE AI Club initiative and a global Kaggle/RSNA competition, our team of four developed a two-stage 3D deep learning pipeline for automated intracranial aneurysm detection in CTA scans. Silent aneurysms can be life-threatening if missed—our goal was to produce reliable scan-level predictions and voxel-wise segmentation masks that help radiologists find them more consistently.

steps.title: Deterministic Geometry Pipeline
steps.description: We built a deterministic preprocessing pipeline that converts 2D DICOM slices into 3D NIfTI volumes, normalizes resolution to 0.5×0.5×1.0 mm, and standardizes volumes to 512×512×256 via center cropping and padding. Targeted ROI extraction across 13 arterial masks focused training on high-relevance vascular structures, cutting noise before the model ever saw a scan.

steps.title: Two-Stage Model & ROSIE Training
steps.description: Stage 1 uses a 3D UNet for voxel-wise segmentation over 128³ volumes; Stage 2 is a lightweight 3D CNN detection head that gates those outputs, suppressing predictions when scan-level probability falls below 50%. Progressive fine-tuning on ROSIE's NVIDIA H100 GPUs (3.2× faster than V100, enabling full-resolution training) raised positive Dice from 0.292 to 0.413, cut healthy-scan false positives to 15.5%, and improved the composite score 59% over baseline—with an AUC of 0.896 (89.6% scan-level detection).

steps.title: Presenting the Research
steps.description: We presented our poster—Evaluation of 3D CNN Architectures for Automated Intracranial Aneurysm Detection in CT Angiography—at MICS 2026 and the MSOE Rosie Competition poster session, sharing methodology and results with researchers and industry visitors.

steps.title: Key Learnings
steps.description: This project taught me how to work with large-scale medical imaging data, from raw DICOM handling to 3D volume construction and model training on high-end GPU hardware. It also strengthened my ability to communicate technical research, both within the team and to external audiences at conferences and competitions.

title: FISH Image Analysis with Mayo Clinic
shortDescription: Partnered with Mayo Clinic in a team of four to automate Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (FISH) image analysis using two deep-learning pipelines—a binary validity classifier and a dual-head probe counter—achieving F1 scores of 0.91 and 0.96.
problemStatement: As part of a semester-long practicum in partnership with Mayo Clinic, our team of four set out to automate Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (FISH) image analysis. FISH is a laboratory technique used to detect genetic abnormalities, but analyzing the resulting images manually is slow and labor-intensive. We tackled the problem with two deep-learning pipelines: a binary validity classifier (Task 1) and a dual-head probe counter (Task 2).

steps.title: Task 1: Binary Validity Classifier
steps.description: For Task 1, I built a hybrid EfficientNetB1 + U-Net CNN in TensorFlow/Keras that takes a FISH nucleus image through convolution and pooling into a fully connected binary output—Marked vs. Rejected. To handle class imbalance I used Binary Focal Cross-Entropy with class weighting and a two-phase schedule (frozen backbone, then full fine-tuning), reaching an F1 of 0.91.

steps.title: Task 2: Dual-Head Probe Counter
steps.description: For Task 2, the model extracts red and green fluorescent signals from each nucleus and predicts probe patterns such as 1R1G. I used a DINOv3 ViT-S/16 backbone with dual CORAL ordinal heads in PyTorch, plus LDAM margins, weighted sampling, and validation-calibrated thresholds to fight severe class imbalance across five pattern classes.

steps.title: HPC Training & Clinical Delivery
steps.description: All models were trained and evaluated on a SLURM-managed multi-GPU HPC cluster. The Task 2 classification report shows strong per-class F1 (0.94–0.97) despite imbalance—e.g. 2R2G at 0.97 F1 with 5,230 support samples—yielding a micro-average F1 of 0.96 and macro-average F1 of 0.95. We delivered reproducible models, predictions, and these performance reports to Mayo Clinic for clinical review.

steps.title: Clinical Patterns & Key Learnings
steps.description: Beyond raw counts, the system must recognize clinically meaningful signal patterns—1R2G, 0R2G, atypical "Other" counts—and reject overcrowded nuclei that cannot be scored reliably. Delivering that end-to-end flow taught me TensorFlow and PyTorch in a clinical setting, imbalance techniques, and how to run reproducible experiments on shared HPC while partnering with Mayo Clinic.

title: Rosie Supercomputer Dashboard
shortDescription: Built a real-time monitoring ecosystem for MSOE's supercomputing cluster with an AI-powered natural language interface, gesture-based kiosk navigation, and containerized deployment.
problemStatement: This was a project for my Software Development Lab at MSOE. I was part of a four-person Scrum team that built a real-time monitoring ecosystem for the university's supercomputing cluster. Our goal was to transform raw hardware data into an intuitive visual experience and an AI-powered natural language interface for students and faculty.

steps.title: System Archaeology & Legacy Recovery
steps.description: One of our first challenges was overcoming significant documentation gaps. We explored the supercomputer's management environment to identify active code paths and resolve repository conflicts. The team reverse-engineered an undocumented Node.js server and successfully fixed a broken Leaderboard API that tracked community resource usage—bringing back rankings for total time used and jobs submitted across hundreds of users.

steps.title: Real-Time Visualization Dashboard
steps.description: We developed a modern web application using Next.js (React 19) and Chakra UI. The home view surfaces cluster context—29 nodes (4 management, 25 compute), 300TB shared storage, 2,128 CPU cores, and a breakdown of teaching, highmem, DGX V100, and DGX H100 nodes—plus the history behind Rosie's name, so students and faculty can understand the system at a glance.

steps.title: NVIDIA Jetson & Kiosk Hardware
steps.description: I took the lead on setting up the NVIDIA Jetson hardware and configuring the Linux environment for our interactive kiosk. This involved getting the Jetson deployment-ready, configuring it for a 4K display, and ensuring reliable operation for the gesture-based interface that ran continuously in the MSOE computing lab.

steps.title: Gesture-Based Navigation
steps.description: We implemented hand-mapping and gestural navigation using a webcam connected to the Jetson hardware. This enabled touchless interaction with the dashboard—users could navigate through metrics, view job statuses, and interact with the system without physically touching the kiosk.

steps.title: AI Chatbot with Llama 3.3
steps.description: We integrated Llama 3.3 running directly on the Rosie supercomputer to power a natural language chatbot. Since the kiosk was a display-only interface without a keyboard, we built speech-to-text capabilities so users could ask questions verbally and receive live metrics, job statuses, and system information through voice interaction.

steps.title: Database & Containerized Deployment
steps.description: The system used a MariaDB database where we executed SQL queries for various data operations including user tracking, job history, and resource metrics. We managed the deployment of web services via Docker and GitLab Container Registries, ensuring seamless updates to the cluster's management nodes.

steps.title: Agile Process & Delivery
steps.description: We shifted toward a 'demo-ready' workflow, breaking down vague requirements into manageable sprint tasks and implementing continuous merging to ensure system stability. We iteratively added features and refined the user experience based on feedback from the MSOE community.

title: Xorbix Innovation Lab Hackathon (3rd Place)
shortDescription: Earned 3rd place building OptiQual—an autonomous visual QA assistant spanning Desktop, Mobile, and Mixed Reality platforms. Used YOLOv11, custom Vision Transformers, and GPT-5 mini to automate defect detection and decision-making.
problemStatement: Xorbix Technologies specializes in providing software solutions across many industries and is the premier Databricks provider in Wisconsin. The challenge was to create a solution that improves Quality Assurance decision-making and defect detection—bridging the gap between detection and actionable recommendations like Rework, Discard, or Escalate.

steps.title: Initial Prototype UI
steps.description: We prototyped a distraction-free operator interface for live inspection. On a conveyor feed, the UI flags defects (e.g. Deformation), lets operators set severity (None / Minor / Major) and defect type, and surfaces an assistant recommendation—such as Rework—with Approve, Reject, and Voice Log controls.

steps.title: AI Pipeline Architecture
steps.description: The core pipeline runs on Databricks: image capture feeds YOLO11 for object detection and cropping, cropped images go to a Vision Transformer for defect classification, and when a defect is found a GPT-5 mini recommendation agent returns an actionable decision. Healthy products short-circuit with a no-defect response so the LLM is only invoked when needed.

steps.title: Live Defect Detection Assistant
steps.description: The desktop application, built with Next.js and TailwindCSS, overlays bounding boxes on the camera feed—green for passing cups, red for defects like actuation or deconstruction. Operators confirm severity and type while the assistant walks through recommendations (e.g. Rework) defect-by-defect with Confirm and Next Defect controls.

steps.title: Object Detection Results
steps.description: YOLO-driven detection was validated across diverse bin samples, classifying materials such as cardboard, book_paper, plastic_loose_bag, plastic_tight_wrap, and plastic_hard with confidence scores—showing the cropping stage could generalize beyond a single product type before ViT defect analysis.

steps.title: Mixed Reality on Meta Quest 3
steps.description: I also started MR development with MRTK3 on a Meta Quest 3 using camera passthrough, so inspectors could view detection results on a body-locked canvas hands-free. Getting YOLO running on-device enabled real-time cropping in the headset without a constant server round-trip for the first detection stage.

steps.title: Innovation Lab Finals & 3rd Place
steps.description: We presented OptiQual at the Innovation Lab 2025 Finals at MSOE, with Databricks sponsorship and a packed auditorium. The team earned 3rd place for tying detection to clear operator actions across desktop, mobile, and mixed reality.

title: Mitchell Park Domes Hackathon (1st Place)
shortDescription: Won 1st place at the MSOE AI Club Hackathon by creating 'The Domes Adventures'—a mobile app that modernizes the visitor experience at the Mitchell Park Domes with AI chatbots, BLE beacon navigation, and interactive scavenger hunts.
problemStatement: The Mitchell Park Domes are a Milwaukee landmark that showcases a variety of plant species from around the world. The challenge was to create a software solution that would increase revenue by enhancing the visitor experience through partnerships, engagement, and accessibility—while being user-friendly and easy to implement.

steps.title: Hackathon Kickoff & Field Trip
steps.description: Hacksgiving kicked off with research and brainstorming, including a free field trip for inspiration before ideas took shape on site. After mapping Domes needs around revenue, engagement, and accessibility, we focused on a mobile app with location-aware content and interactive features for the Arid, Tropical, and Floral Domes.

steps.title: Rapid Development & App Home
steps.description: We built the app in React Native and Expo with extreme programming—UX and backend in parallel. The home screen welcomes visitors and lets them choose an environment to explore, with bottom navigation for Voice Chat, Botanical Tales, Dome Detective, and Sip & Seek.

steps.title: BLE Beacon Integration
steps.description: A core technical challenge was Bluetooth Low Energy beacon tracking to unlock progressive Botanical Tales audio as visitors move through the facility. We programmed ESP32 microcontrollers and integrated them with the React Native app to deliver location-based content seamlessly.

steps.title: Sip & Seek Partnerships
steps.description: Sip & Seek ties the café to the exhibit: purchase a beverage, scan the barcode, and unlock a custom drink adventure. Featured drinks like the Prickly Pear Cooler and Hibiscus Paradise map ingredients back to plants in the Domes—creating a partnership loop that supports engagement and revenue.

steps.title: On-Site Testing at the Domes
steps.description: We tested Dome Detective on site—an AI scavenger hunt that validates finds with vision and LLM checks. Real-world trials confirmed the models stayed grounded and that the experience felt engaging for visitors, which shaped our final UX polish.

steps.title: Accessibility & Multilingual Support
steps.description: Inclusivity was a core design goal. The app offers accessibility controls, language switching, and voice chat, with a full Spanish UI (Accesibilidad, Idioma, and localized nav labels like Cuentos Botánicos and Detective de Domos)—supporting a diverse visitor base beyond a single language.

steps.title: Presentation & 1st Place Win
steps.description: We presented at MSOE, focusing on revenue strategies and the Domes' future. The team was awarded 1st place for balancing technical innovation with practical business value for Friends of the Domes / Milwaukee Domes Alliance visitors.

title: Personal Portfolio Website
shortDescription: A responsive website built with TypeScript, React, and Tailwind CSS to showcase my work and skills. Designed with a clean, modern aesthetic and modular code for scalability and performance.
problemStatement: My personal portfolio was a solo project designed not just to showcase my technical skills, but also to challenge myself with new technologies and design philosophies. I built it over the course of two months using TypeScript, React, and Tailwind CSS.

steps.title: Technology & Deployment
steps.description: This project served as an introduction to TypeScript—a typed superset of JavaScript widely used in modern development—and gave me hands-on experience with React's component-based architecture. I initially deployed the site with AWS Amplify, then migrated hosting to GitHub Pages and added a GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline that builds, tests, and deploys on every push. That migration taught me how to own the full delivery path—from cloud hosting tradeoffs to automated checks and production deploys. The primary goal was to create a clean, responsive, and professional space where I could display my projects, resume, and personal background.

steps.title: UX-First Design
steps.description: I approached the design with a UX-first mindset, drawing from principles I learned in my classes as well as graphic design experience from high school. I wanted the site to be intuitive, fast, and aesthetically pleasing across devices. Tailwind CSS played a key role in this, helping me write maintainable styles without bloating the codebase. With Tailwind's utility classes, I was able to create sleek layouts, manage spacing, and apply responsive designs all within my React components. I also focused on accessibility and performance, using semantic HTML and optimized routing.

steps.title: Component Architecture
steps.description: One of the major lessons from this project was how to manage frontend logic through reusable components and state management. I built modular sections for each page—such as the Projects page with interactive previews and links to detailed writeups—and ensured that routing between pages was smooth and consistent. I also implemented a footer "Contact Me" section on every page for easy access to my email and LinkedIn. As the site grew, I began to think more critically about file organization, component responsibility, and scalability, which are essential skills for frontend developers.

steps.title: Key Learnings
steps.description: This project was both challenging and incredibly rewarding. Seeing real-time changes and iterating on design elements helped me appreciate the power and flexibility of modern frontend tools. It also gave me a better understanding of how developers work with both logic and layout simultaneously. Beyond the technical skills, it taught me how to present my work thoughtfully and communicate my experiences effectively—something that's essential in both job applications and team environments.

title: Travel Dataset Analysis
shortDescription: Collaborated on analyzing a travel dataset using both supervised and unsupervised machine learning models. Focused on extracting insights, evaluating performance, and communicating findings through a detailed report.
problemStatement: In this data science project, my partner and I explored a massive dataset containing over 100,000 entries related to global travel trends. Our goal was to uncover patterns and test hypotheses using both supervised and unsupervised machine learning models.

steps.title: Data Preprocessing
steps.description: From the beginning, we treated data preprocessing as a crucial phase—handling missing values, encoding categorical variables, scaling features, and performing exploratory data analysis using visualizations. This step gave us a solid foundation for building effective models and ensured the integrity of our insights.

steps.title: Supervised Learning
steps.description: We implemented two supervised learning models: K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) and Logistic Regression. KNN allowed us to make predictions based on proximity in feature space, making it great for identifying clusters of traveler behaviors, while Logistic Regression gave us probability-based classifications which we used to predict outcomes like travel destination type or likelihood of returning customers. Both models were rigorously evaluated using accuracy, precision, and recall, and we also plotted decision boundaries to visualize how the models performed in different feature spaces.

steps.title: Unsupervised Learning
steps.description: For the unsupervised portion of the project, we applied K-Means Clustering to identify latent groupings within the dataset. By experimenting with different values of k and analyzing the resulting inertia and silhouette scores, we were able to segment traveler types into meaningful clusters. These clusters helped support some of our initial hypotheses and led to the discovery of unexpected trends—such as seasonal spikes in certain demographic behaviors or destination preferences based on booking lead time. We compiled our entire workflow, findings, and visualizations into a polished report that explained both our methodology and our conclusions.

steps.title: Key Learnings
steps.description: This project significantly deepened my understanding of the machine learning pipeline—from data cleaning to model evaluation—and helped sharpen my skills with Python's data ecosystem. Working in a Jupyter Notebook environment encouraged iterative development and visualization-heavy analysis, which is ideal for data science. Most importantly, the experience gave me confidence in making data-driven decisions, collaborating on technical documents, and translating complex findings into digestible insights.

title: Wordle Application
shortDescription: A JavaFX-based game inspired by Wordle, developed in a team of five using Scrum methodology. Features include word validation, feedback logic, and a polished graphical interface.
problemStatement: During a semester-long software engineering course, I collaborated with four peers to develop a fully functional Wordle-style game using Java and JavaFX. From the outset, we designed the project with real-world software development in mind.

steps.title: Scrum & Collaboration
steps.description: We implemented the Scrum methodology, organizing our work into sprints and holding regular stand-ups to assess progress and adjust goals. GitLab served as our version control system, allowing us to manage collaboration effectively, merge branches seamlessly, and track development history. These practices mimicked industry standards and taught us how to manage team dynamics and development workflows in a professional setting.

steps.title: Core Gameplay & UI
steps.description: The foundational version of the game replicated the classic Wordle gameplay, including proper input validation, feedback mechanisms for guesses, and robust win/loss logic. However, we didn't stop there. Our team collectively decided to take on optional extension tasks that pushed the game to become something more. We refined the UI using JavaFX to deliver a clean, responsive, and engaging user interface that felt modern and intuitive. Attention was given to color schemes, animations, and layout consistency to enhance user experience.

steps.title: Advanced Features
steps.description: To differentiate our project further, we introduced unique game modes and administrative tools. We built an "Evil Wordle" mode, where the game dynamically changed the target word to maximize difficulty and prolong the game—similar to the concept of "Evil Hangman." We also added AI players capable of playing the game algorithmically and created an admin panel that allowed advanced control over gameplay settings, logs, and user sessions. These features not only showcased our creativity but also challenged us to think about game design, logic branching, and state management on a much deeper level.

steps.title: Key Learnings
steps.description: This project was a major stepping stone in my development journey. I learned how to apply object-oriented programming in a practical context and how to manage feature creep while preserving code quality. The SCRUM process gave me a strong foundation in agile methodology, and our frequent retrospectives helped improve both technical implementation and team communication. The experience also gave me confidence in building GUI applications and affirmed my interest in collaborative, structured development environments.
